message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: No lockfile / constraints / hashes — supply-chain audit and installs are non-reproducible
DIMENSION: dependency & supply chain
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: pyproject.toml 38-74
DESCRIPTION: Pass-1 finding #8 added pip-audit to CI but left the reproducibility half of its own suggested fix unaddressed: there is still NO lockfile (no uv.lock/poetry.lock), NO constraints*.txt, NO requirements*.txt, and the audit job runs `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` resolving fresh versions every run. I confirmed the heavy extras pull a large, fast-moving surface (217+ installed dists; current resolution includes cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, urllib3 2.7.0, Werkzeug 3.1.8, Flask 3.1.3, grpcio 1.81.1, protobuf 6.33.6, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51). Because nothing is pinned, (a) the pip-audit result is a point-in-time snapshot that can differ between two CI runs on the same commit, (b) a green audit today gives no guarantee about the artifact a user installs tomorrow, and (c) pip-audit's hash/pinned-aware checks (`--require-hashes`, `--locked`) cannot be used. pip-audit clean today (OSV and PyPI both: 'No known vulnerabilities found') — but that is exactly the transient guarantee the lockfile gap undermines.
SUGGESTED FIX: Commit a locked/constraints file for the heavy lanes (e.g. `uv pip compile` or `pip-compile` output, or `uv.lock`) and have the audit job install from it and run `pip-audit -r <locked-file> --require-hashes -S`, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers. This makes both installs and the advisory scan reproducible.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding about lockfile/constraints/hashes for reproducible installs and supply-chain auditing.

content.content:
1	[build-system]
2	requires = ["hatchling"]
3	build-backend = "hatchling.build"
4	
5	[project]
6	name = "assay-engine"
7	dynamic = ["version"]  # single-sourced from src/assay_engine/__init__.py:__version__ (#120)
8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "thehipsterciso" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
19	    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
20	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
21	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
22	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
23	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
24	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
25	    "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering",
26	    "Topic :: Security",
27	    "Typing :: Typed",
28	]
29	# The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are optional extras (lazy-imported,
30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay"
35	Repository = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay"
36	Issues = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay/issues"
37	
38	[project.optional-dependencies]
39	dev = [
40	    "pytest>=8",
41	    "pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)
42	    "ruff>=0.6",
43	    "mypy>=1.11",
44	]
45	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
46	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
47	reasoning = [
48	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
49	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
50	    "anyio>=4,<5",
51	]
52	observability = [
53	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
54	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
55	    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",
56	    "openinference-instrumentation-langchain>=0.1.40,<1",
57	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",
58	]
59	persistence = [
60	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",  # core — provides MemorySaver (declared, not left transitive)
61	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
62	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
63	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",
64	]
65	orchestration = [
66	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",
67	]
68	baseline = [
69	    "numpy>=1.26,<3",  # vectorized similarity at corpus scale (pure-Python fallback otherwise)
70	]
71	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
72	all = [
73	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",
74	]
75	
76	[tool.hatch.version]
77	path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"
78	
79	[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
80	packages = ["src/assay_engine"]
81	
82	[tool.pytest.ini_options]
83	testpaths = ["tests"]
84	pythonpath = ["src"]
85	
86	[tool.ruff]
87	line-length = 100
88	src = ["src", "tests"]
89	
90	[tool.mypy]
91	# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI
92	# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
93	packages = ["assay_engine"]
94	strict = true
95	
96	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
97	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
98	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
99	module = [
100	    "langchain_ollama.*",
101	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
102	    "anyio.*",
103	    "opentelemetry.*",
104	    "phoenix.*",
105	    "mlflow.*",
106	    "openinference.*",
107	    "langgraph.*",
108	    "qdrant_client.*",
109	    "psycopg.*",
110	    "psycopg_pool.*",
111	    "numpy.*",
112	]
113	ignore_missing_imports = true
114

content.text: No lockfile/constraints/requirements files in the repo root. Let me check the CI workflow and the pip-audit configuration to understand the claim about the audit job.

content.content:
1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74	
75	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
76	  # with advisories). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id per line) covers
77	  # accepted/unfixable ones. (#108)
78	  audit:
79	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
80	    steps:
81	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
82	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
83	        with:
84	          python-version: "3.12"
85	      - name: Install (all extras) + pip-audit
86	        run: |
87	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit
88	          pip install -e ".[dev,all]"
89	      - name: pip-audit (known-vulnerability scan)
90	        run: |
91	          IGNORES=""
92	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
93	            while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done < .pip-audit-ignore
94	          fi
95	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES
96

content.text: The CI audit job indeed runs `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` resolving fresh each run, with no lockfile/constraints/hashes. The finding is factually accurate. Now let me assess whether this is a genuine production-readiness defect or documented/justified scope. Let me check ADRs and any documented decisions on dependency strategy.

content.content:
docs/CHARTER.md:11:**rigorous, reproducible empirical machine-learning and NLP analysis of security & privacy
docs/CHARTER.md:23:2. **Reproducible by a hostile reviewer.** Every finding is reconstructable from published
docs/CHARTER.md:50:**In scope:** an analytical engine and methodology; the governance, reproducibility, and
docs/CHARTER.md:71:- a complete, publishable reproducibility package.
docs/GOVERNANCE.md:1:# hc-assay — Governance, Reproducibility & Independence
docs/GOVERNANCE.md:87:reproducibility and of the independence claim.
docs/GOVERNANCE.md:119:- **A complete reproducibility package** published with findings.
docs/GOVERNANCE.md:130:## 6. Reproducibility guarantees (summary)
docs/METHODOLOGY.md:126:## 7. Reproducibility
docs/METHODOLOGY.md:133:- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:19:| 8 | MEDIUM | ops-packaging-supplychain | No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine | `ci.yml` 8 |
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:31:| 20 | LOW | ops-packaging-supplychain | Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest | `pyproject.toml` 7 |
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:114:- **Verifier 1:** The mechanism is real: src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112 (`return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]`) boxes the dense numpy similarity matrix into a nested Python list, and the public API (cosine_similarity_matrix, line 115) is contractually list[list[float]] with no streaming/top-k/blocked alternative, so a full O(n^2) dense matrix is always materialized regardless of caller need. My PoC confirms the boxing loop (0.46s) dwarfs the actual vectorized numeric work (0.019s) at n=3000, and the nested list does cost ~4x an equivalent ndarray.\n\nBut the finding is materially over-stated, and its severity rests on a flawed benchmark:\n\n1) The "~70x peak memory" headline and "~576 MB nested list" are wrong. Measured deep size of the returned nested list at n=3000,d=384 is 294 MB vs 72 MB for the equivalent ndarray = 4.1x, not 70x. (4.1x is the textbook boxed-float-vs-float64 factor.) The "70x" appears to conflate per-cell byte ratios with something else; it is not reproducible.\n\n2) The "7.76 s" runtime (and "minutes of boxing on a Mac mini") is a tracemalloc artifact. Under tracemalloc the function takes ~7.9s; with tracemalloc off, clean wall-clock is 0.33s at n=3000 an
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:140:### 8. [MEDIUM] No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:142:- **Problem:** Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a transitive version with a freshly-disclosed CVE and CI will never notice. For a tool that brands itself for security & privacy data and is being hardened for production, the absence of an advisory-database check (and of a lockfile / constraints file for the heavy lanes) is a genuine supply-chain gap rather than a style preference.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:143:- **Suggested fix:** Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:144:- **Verifier 1:** Confirmed as a genuine production-readiness ops/supply-chain gap, not a false positive or a justified design choice.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:148:2. `grep -rniE 'pip-audit|safety|bandit|cyclonedx|osv|trivy|grype'` across .github/, pyproject.toml, docs/, scripts/, CONTRIBUTING.md returns nothing relevant (only unrelated "auditable provenance" mentions). The dev extra (pyproject lines 39-43) is just pytest/ruff/mypy.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:149:3. No lockfile or constraints file exists anywhere in the repo (searched for *.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt). Dependencies use floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. cryptography is not even directly declared — it is an unconstrained transitive; aiohttp/requests/urllib3 likewise transitive).
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:151:- **Verifier 2:** Independently confirmed. The repo has exactly one CI workflow (/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml) and a repo-wide grep for pip-audit|safety|bandit|trivy|snyk|osv|dependabot across all yml/yaml/toml/txt/cfg/ini files returns nothing. There is no .github/dependabot.yml, no lockfile (uv.lock), no constraints*.txt, and no requirements*.txt anywhere in the working tree (the only "audit"/".lock" hits are git branch refs and transcript JS filenames). So no dependency-vulnerability scanning and no pinned/reproducible install exist for the heavy lanes.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:157:- **Problem:** GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for the abort. PoC against the venv (BadParser raising during INGEST, caller-owned trail): the trail ends at seq 0 'run_start' and seq 1 'phase enter INGEST', then stops. An auditor reading the trail alone cannot distinguish "run aborted at ingest because the parser hit disk corruption" from "run is still in progress" or "run completed but stopped logging" — the single most diagnostic fact (what failed and why) is absent precisely on the failure path the partial-trail feature exists to serve. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of diagnosing a blocked run; a silent truncation is an audit hole, not an audit record.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:247:- **Problem:** METHODOLOGY.md §6 and the fence.py docstring state the fence "refuses at construction to hold an Interpretation (even nested)" and the Measurement.__post_init__ comment claims it is blocked "at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22, #30)." The recursive guard _contains_interpretation does NOT honor that for two cases, both reproducible against the venv:
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:270:- **Verifier 1:** The technical claim is correct and reproducible. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py, `_submit_bounded` (lines 147-165) wraps `_pool.submit(fn)` in `try/except BaseException` solely to roll back the `_inflight` counter, then re-raises the original exception raw (line 163). When the shared module-global ThreadPoolExecutor (`_pool`, line 136) is shut down, `submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`, which is NOT a member of the documented `ReasoningError` hierarchy (note: `ReasoningError` subclasses `RuntimeError`, but a plain `RuntimeError` is not a `ReasoningError`, so the `except ReasoningError` clauses in `_run_with_retries` lines 448-459 do not catch it). I verified the leak at three levels: `_with_timeout`, `_submit_bounded`, and the public `TieredReasoningSeam.run()` — in all cases a bare `RuntimeError` escapes after `seam._pool.shutdown()`. A caller catching only the documented `ReasoningError` hierarchy would miss it. So the finding is genuine.\n\nSeverity is recalibrated from MEDIUM down to LOW because the trigger window is narrow and not exercised by normal operation: I grepped the whole src tree and there is NO explicit `_pool.shutdown(
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:271:- **Verifier 2:** The finding is technically accurate and reproducible. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py, `_submit_bounded` (line 147-165) calls `_pool.submit(fn)` at line 159 inside a `try/except BaseException` that only decrements `_inflight` and re-raises the raw exception (lines 160-163). If the module-global `_pool` (line 136) is shut down, `ThreadPoolExecutor.submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`, which is NOT in the documented error hierarchy. Critically, this `RuntimeError` is the PARENT of `ReasoningError` (line 65: `class ReasoningError(RuntimeError)`), so the retry loop's `except ReasoningError` clauses in `_run_with_retries` (lines 448-459) do NOT catch it — it propagates straight out of the public `TieredReasoningSeam.run()`. The module docstring and class docstrings advertise `PermanentReasoningError`/`RateLimitError`/`ReasoningError` as the contract, so a caller catching that hierarchy would miss this. The suggested fix (normalize submit-time RuntimeError to PermanentReasoningError) is correct and trivial.\n\nSeverity is recalibrated DOWN from MEDIUM to LOW because the trigger surface is very narrow: I confirmed via grep that the reasoning `_

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:319:### 20. [LOW] Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:321:- **Problem:** `pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A release that bumps only one of the two strings (the common failure mode) ships a wheel whose distributed version disagrees with the version recorded in every provenance manifest it produces — silently undermining the project's central reproducibility guarantee while passing all tests.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:327:3. The module `__version__` is consumed by the reproducibility harness: determinism.py:24 imports it as `_ENGINE_VERSION` and line 111 stamps it as `component_versions["engine"]` in the DeterminismRecord.

The one genuine direction it could catch (constructs valid on 3.11 but removed in 3.14, e.g. typing.ByteString) did not even reproduce — mypy/typeshed flagged nothing at either target. Most decisively, the actual codebase only uses stable typing constructs (Any, Callable, Mapping,

It is not already-handled: there is no per-version `mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}` invocation anywhere (grep found none).

content.text:
All claims confirmed:
- No lockfile/constraints/requirements anywhere in repo.
- CI audit job (and integration job) install with `pip install -e ".[...]"`, resolving fresh every run.
- The heavy CVE-surface deps (cryptography, aiohttp, requests, urllib3, Werkzeug, Flask, grpcio, protobuf, openai, anthropic, boto3, SQLAlchemy) are entirely undeclared transitives — fully unconstrained, can float to any version satisfying the extras.
- pip-audit supports `--locked`, `--require-hashes`, `-r` — none usable without a lockfile.
- Pass-1 #8 explicitly raised the lockfile half as part of the fix and it was deliberately left unaddressed (the fix commit `d9e5399` added only pip-audit).

Now let me calibrate. Is this a documented/justified scope decision, or a genuine gap? Let me check whether project governance/methodology makes any reproducibility commitments that the environment (not just the engine source) feeds into.

123	> is expected. The engine enforces the direction structurally at the type boundary; it does not,

126	## 7. Reproducibility
127	
128	- **Determinism:** fixed, documented seeds; inputs hashed; model names + versions recorded.
129	- **Provenance:** an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes.
130	- **Pre-registration:** hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and
131	  RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation. This is honestly described as an adaptive
132	  design with a timestamped lock — not as predictions guessed before any data.
133	- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
134	  findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.
135	
136	## 8. Scoped-independence caveat
137	
138	When the baseline is built from text or artifacts that an external source also authored,
139	the baseline is independent of that source's **judgments**, but not of its **wording or
140	framing**. This is an acceptable, necessary compromise — but it is claimed as *scoped*
141	independence, stated explicitly, and is one of the reasons `indeterminate` exists.

content.text:
I have enough to assess. Let me form the final judgment.

The methodology promises reproducibility of *findings* via published data/code/config/logs and records "model names + versions." Note that this is at the level of the published reproducibility package, not necessarily the CI/install environment. This is an important nuance for severity calibration: the project's reproducibility guarantees are about reconstructing decision history from published artifacts, not about bit-reproducible installs. The lockfile gap is a real supply-chain/CI hygiene gap, but it does not break the documented reproducibility-of-findings guarantee (which captures versions at runtime into the determinism record).

My assessment:

**The finding is TRUE and accurate.** Every factual claim verified:
- No lockfile/constraints/requirements anywhere (confirmed via filesystem search).
- CI audit + integration jobs install via `pip install -e ".[...]"` with fresh resolution every run.
- Heavy CVE-surface deps are undeclared, fully unconstrained transitives.
- pip-audit supports `--locked`/`--require-hashes`/`-r` — unusable without a lockfile.
- The installed surface matches (230 dists; exact versions cited match the venv).
- Pass-1 #8 explicitly flagged the lockfile half as part of its own fix and it was deliberately left unaddressed (only pip-audit was added in commit `d9e5399`) — so this is "anything pass-1 missed," legitimately in scope.

**Severity: MEDIUM is correct, not higher.** The audit being a point-in-time snapshot is a genuine weakness for a security/privacy-branded production engine. But: (a) the engine core is dependency-free (only consumers of heavy *extras* are exposed); (b) library consumers benefit from the permissive ranges, so the lockfile belongs only to the CI/heavy lanes; (c) no actual vulnerability exists today; (d) the project's documented reproducibility-of-findings guarantee is at the published-package level and records runtime versions independently, so the gap doesn't violate a stated guarantee — it's defense-in-depth/ops hygiene. Not a hard-constraint violation. MEDIUM is well-calibrated.

input.reasoning:
VERIFIED TRUE. Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the real repo, and it correctly identifies a gap that pass-1 #8 explicitly named but deliberately left unaddressed (so it is in-scope: "anything pass-1 missed," not a re-report of a FIXED item).

What pass-1 actually did: commit d9e5399 ("fix(harden-1): ops/supply-chain batch #108,#120,#121") added the pip-audit CI job. But pass-1 #8's own suggested fix (docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:143) had two halves: "Add a CI job that runs pip-audit ..." AND "Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. pip-compile output or uv.lock) ... so installs are reproducible and audited." Only the first half was implemented. The reproducibility half was never done. This is therefore a legitimate pass-1 miss, not a duplicate.

Confirmed facts:
1) No lockfile/constraints/requirements anywhere. Filesystem search for *.lock, uv.lock, poetry.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt across the working tree returns nothing (only .venv internals).
2) CI installs resolve fresh every run. .github/workflows/ci.yml line 88 (audit job) runs `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` and line 65 (integration job) runs the full-extras install; neither pins. The audit step (line 95) runs bare `pip-audit` against that freshly-resolved env.
3) Heavy CVE-surface deps are entirely undeclared, fully unconstrained transitives. grep of pyproject.toml for cryptography|aiohttp|requests|urllib3|werkzeug|flask|grpcio|protobuf|openai|anthropic|boto3|sqlalchemy returns NONE — they enter only transitively through the extras and can float to any version.
4) The resolved surface matches the finding's numbers: 230 installed dists in the venv; cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, urllib3 2.7.0, Werkzeug 3.1.8, Flask 3.1.3, grpcio 1.81.1, protobuf 6.33.6, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51 — all confirmed present at exactly those versions.
5) pip-audit 2.10.1 (the version present) supports --locked, --require-hashes, and -r/--requirement — none of which can be used without a committed lockfile/hashed requirements, exactly as the finding states. So the strongest, reproducible mode of the very tool pass-1 added is unavailable.
6) The .pip-audit-ignore allowlist the audit step references is conditional (`if [ -f ... ]`) and does not exist in the repo — minor, but confirms the audit lane is loosely wired.

Consequences are real: (a) the pip-audit result is a point-in-time snapshot that can differ between two CI runs on the same commit; (b) a green audit today gives no guarantee about the artifact a user installs tomorrow; (c) the reproducible/hardened audit modes are unusable. For an engine that brands itself for security/privacy data and is being hardened for production, this is a genuine supply-chain/ops gap, not style.

Severity = MEDIUM (confirming the assessor; not higher). Reasons it is not HIGH: (a) the engine core is dependency-free (dependencies = []); only consumers who install the heavy *extras* are exposed, so the lockfile belongs to the CI/heavy lanes only and pyproject ranges should stay permissive for library consumers (the assessor's fix respects this). (b) pip-audit is clean today (no live vulnerability). (c) The project's documented reproducibility guarantee (METHODOLOGY.md §7, GOVERNANCE.md §6) is reproducibility-of-FINDINGS via a published reproducibility package, and component/library versions are recorded at runtime into the DeterminismRecord (baseline/determinism.py component_versions) independently of how CI installs — so this gap is defense-in-depth/CI hygiene, NOT a violation of a stated guarantee. (d) It does not touch any hard constraint (loopback-only, no metered API, engine-imports-no-adapter, lazy heavy backends are all unrelated). Not below MEDIUM because the audit-snapshot non-determinism materially weakens the supply-chain assurance pass-1 set out to provide for a security-positioned production engine.
input.evidence:
FILES: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml (deps=[] line 31; extras lines 38-74; heavy CVE-surface deps NOT declared — all transitive), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (audit job lines 78-95: `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` line 88 + bare `pip-audit` line 95; integration job full-extras install line 65), /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:143 (pass-1 #8 suggested fix names the lockfile half as "Consider also..." — left unimplemented), commit d9e5399 (added only pip-audit).

REPRO/PoC:
- `find` for *.lock/uv.lock/poetry.lock/constraints*.txt/requirements*.txt across working tree -> none.
- grep pyproject.toml for cryptography|aiohttp|requests|urllib3|werkzeug|flask|grpcio|protobuf|openai|anthropic|boto3|sqlalchemy -> NONE declared (all unconstrained transitives).
- `.venv/bin/pip list | wc -l` -> 232 lines (~230 dists). Key versions confirmed: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, urllib3 2.7.0, Werkzeug 3.1.8, Flask 3.1.3, grpcio 1.81.1, protobuf 6.33.6, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51.
- `.venv/bin/pip-audit --help` -> 2.10.1 supports --locked, --require-hashes, -r/--requirement (all unusable without a committed lockfile/hashed requirements).
- .pip-audit-ignore referenced conditionally in ci.yml but absent from repo.

SUGGESTED FIX (sound, scope-correct): commit a locked/hashed constraints file for the heavy lanes (uv pip compile / pip-compile / uv.lock), install from it in the CI audit (and integration) job, and run `pip-audit -r <locked> --require-hashes` (or `--locked`), while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers. Makes both installs and the advisory scan reproducible.

content.text: Verified the finding independently. It is TRUE and MEDIUM severity is correctly calibrated. Summary returned via StructuredOutput: every factual claim checks out (no lockfile/constraints/requirements anywhere; CI audit + integration jobs resolve dependencies fresh each run; the heavy CVE-surface deps are entirely undeclared/unconstrained transitives; pip-audit's `--locked`/`--require-hashes`/`-r` modes are unusable without a lockfile; the 230-dist surface and exact versions match the venv). It is legitimately in-scope rather than a re-report — pass-1 #8 explicitly named the lockfile half as part of its own fix (docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:143) and only the pip-audit half was implemented (commit d9e5399). Kept at MEDIUM rather than higher because the engine core is dependency-free, no live vulnerability exists today, and the project's documented reproducibility-of-findings guarantee captures versions at runtime independently of CI install resolution.
